Okay, well, we are at the top of the hour here, so let's get started. I am Eileen Cartwright. I'll be the moderator for today's Influence Q&A session. Hosting, we have bestselling author and CEO of the Chaldeany Institute, Boss Walters. So get your questions ready. We do have to have a hard stop on the hour. As always, before we begin, please have your microphones muted unless you are called on. Feel free to use the chat area to send any questions you have, or you can raise your hand using the feature at the bottom of the screen under reactions. So without further ado, take it away, Boss. Yes, welcome everybody. Good to see you all. Great. I see very a lot of familiar faces here. Jason, Martin, John, Paul. That's good to see you. Albert, nice to see you, Walter. Yes, so this is indeed, like Eileen said, Q&A session. So I am here to help. So I'm here to answer all your questions regarding the topic of the science of ethical influence. And I see the first hand is raised. So let's kick off, Eileen. Yeah, let's start off with Jason. Go ahead, Jason, unmute yourself and ask away. Thank you. I have a burning question about the principle of authority. The two, I guess the amplifiers are expertise and trustworthiness. But my question is, is I've uncovered in some of my reading that the idea about the authority and the characteristics of authority as being credible, and the credible authority or the credible source in other documents includes a third amplifier, which is the demonstration of good will. And I'm just curious about what your thought is on that and its role in the credibility of the person or the credible source. And I'm just curious if it does have a role, why have you not included it in the material? I've used it in my training for years and I always thought like in my head, I was like, okay, I'm pretty sure the source had to go digging for it again because when I saw your content and didn't include it, I'm like, okay, where did I get it from? So I'm just curious about a, is it something that is included in the idea of credibility or is it in a different context of what the principle of authority is from the Childini Institute? I hope that makes sense. Yeah, it makes sense, Jason. And great question. Thanks for asking. And indeed, so the activator of the principle of authority is being the credible communicator. And what we also say, and what research backs up is that if people perceive you as the authority, you are the most powerful communicator that you can be. And your credibility is built upon your expertise and your trustworthiness. So that's how authority is built up from the activator's point of view. If I understand your question correctly, it's a sign of goodwill. I hear something that I connect with cooperation, which is an activator of liking. You have similarities, praise and cooperation. So your willingness to work with somebody show that you have the interest of the other person in mind leads to liking, but it there is connected to that principle. And therefore it's not connected to become a more credible, more expertise transfer of information. So you're saying of goodwill, it doesn't mean you are an expert. No, I know that it's just that some of the literature on a credible source actually parses out the three things, trustworthiness, expertise and goodwill as being the three things are being a credible source. And it's in some of the textbooks on influence and social psychology and I was just curious about how it fits. So are you suggesting that it fits better under the liking principle under cooperation? So being of goodwill and indeed under cooperation, that will be my first reply. I don't know, Eilidh, if you have a take on that specific question of Jason. I would agree that it would be more of a liking. I'm not familiar with that the goodwill being a part of other social psychologists research and information. So I'd have to check that out. But so but from our standpoint, the credibility is the amplifier of authority and goodwill. Yeah, I would have to really read that research. If you could send that over that would be. I can. I can for sure. I can for sure. And it's not. What is your take? What how do you think it would? Well, I guess I've taken it. I've lumped it into the category of being a credible source of information in the context of being a credible authority. And when we teach case managers, we say, you have to have expertise in this and you have to display trustworthiness by doing these things. But also there's a demonstration of goodwill. As in the doing what's in the best interest of that person doesn't mean giving it to them, but like making sure they're looked after like in the context of the industry at work. And so that's where we've used it. And and it's come out in some of the qualitative work that we've done when we've done focus groups and in depth interviews, where they talk about this person as being credible, obviously with expertise and and being trustworthy, but then they'll also give stories about like, they kind of looked after me like my claim was coming to an end and they made sure I was looked after so like they were credible in my eyes like they had my best interest. And so that's when I did the when I was doing some of the digging and research, trying to find out, well, what does that actually mean? There is some literature and I'll find it for you for sure and I'll just send it off and you guys can just take a look at it. But that's what my take was and we just lump it in there. And he's buying other companies and these companies don't buy the local bakery. They buy pretty substantial companies, of course. So the other person is also a pretty B2B person. And so it takes too long after and that part I will do a short version. Some studies recommendation come from Dr. Nydert and he says to the CEO of the Fortune 100 company, what I like you to do. I created a list of 10 things you like to talk about and 10 things the other CEO likes to talk about. I want you at the beginning of that meeting, start talking about one or two or three of the subjects, but for about 15 minutes. The CEO first refused. He says time is money. We need to talk about business. And Dr. Nydert was luckily pretty persuasive and the CEO, the Fortune 100 company agreed to do it. What happened? First conversation. That's a funny story. He didn't talk. It was about sailing. And they talked not for 15 minutes, but for 45 minutes until the rest of the room start saying, well, should we start talking about business? What was the effect? A normal process of merchant acquisition took about nine months. This single change brought that back to six months, saving per acquisition between 10 and 30 million dollars. In the next 10 rounds of buying new companies, this saved over 200 million dollars. Well, more B2B setting, you don't get it. It's about a luxury sales. For example, sales of luxury real estate. I have a question about pricing. What would be the best pricing strategy for luxury sales to use precise numbers because we talked a lot that precise numbers they create trust. Or for luxury sales, we do around numbers because they're perceived more easily. And I remember that in one of our masterminds, you mentioned a special study and I couldn't find it about luxury sales. Could you please share this? Yeah, yeah, for sure. Thanks for asking. And indeed, luxury items. And I think I mentioned in that mastermind don't confuse luxury items with expensive offers. A luxury item is a Louis Vuitton bag for $5,000. But a Toyota car for $40,000 isn't a luxury item. So it's not only about the amount of money that is involved, what is perceived as a luxury item. So indeed, a Louis Vuitton bag research shows you price it at $5,000 because that is a much easier to perceive the amount of money and research shows what I'm aware of. That's the best strategy. I think you're talking about luxury real estate. Again, if I imagine now luxury, luxury villas, multimillion dollar properties, round numbers would definitely be my advice there because that's perceived again as a luxury item. So round numbers in the pricing, although if you come to negotiation, now exact numbers could work to get a better outcome because now in a negotiation things could matter. So if you would say, let's say you are selling it and you say, well, the villas 10 million and they come back, well, I want to pay 9 million. So well, based on my financial situation, I just need to have 9.5 million, 5403,371 cents. That would turn the negotiation in your benefit because suddenly you really thought about your offer coming back. So your initial opening price, you don't want to be a very exact number because that would move people away from you. It has to be easily processable numbers as a luxury item. If you start, which always happens normally in a negotiation, then exact numbers could turn the negotiation more in your benefit. That would be my recommendation. Okay, thank you, Bas. He says that if you're going to use scarcity as a way of selling them. It's important you determine what sort of scarcity is appropriate. So is it supply. There's not much supply. Is there too much demand either the two left or is it a time scarcity. And he's got lots of research to show that if you're dealing with luxury goods. It's as supply as you and boss mentioned the glorious house or something like that obviously works. But if you're talking about an expensive goods. Like a car as opposed to a luxury car. I was going to go down the seven principles and trying to determine if there was a dominant logos ethos or pathos in each of the seven principles. I think liking I think was pure pathos unity is pure pathos social belief I think is logos and pathos authority of course is almost certainly ethos consistency is logical logos and scarcity is logos and pathos so what I'm trying to say is whether you agree those categories or not or whether you don't even know what I'm talking about. What to me is so interesting is that Chaldean is taking age old ideas and synthesizing them brilliantly as always and you can actually see sort of ancient wisdom hidden in his modern concepts and I think that's a for me it's a really important point and and again I'm not saying all my categories are correct but for me it's it's there's a lot of distilled ancient wisdom in what we've all been learning on the course and in the books and so on and so forth more recently. I was able to help them and do something special for their clients because again they look good. Then probably the client comes back to those lawyers or financial advisors. Wow, Mark was great. I think that's the second thing. I would say that if you feel good about themself and about you and now you have like an ongoing upward spiral of all the time getting these referrals. So this that's the second thing. So I would say can you go from a gift perspective to them instead of you have to be a client and then I will give you business. I will give you the feedback on the science. They will reciprocate. Second, when they do make sure you give feedback. Tell them what you do for their clients and go the extra mile in the beginning days because they will hear it not only from you, but probably also from their clients, which is a motivator to give you the next client. So they trust themselves. So the principle of consistency is a powerful principle, the higher, the more high again, the more responsibility the stakeholder has, because they are in that position for a certain reason. And in their mind, often the reasoning is they are in that position because they made successful choices. So I would look and do my homework in this case, which previous commitments these stakeholders made already before your interview that support your goal.
